title:
I find the constant invocation of whether a Prime Minister is a Churchill or not so tired. 

At this point it feels like we are a culture invoking the attributes of a valorous Saint or the qualities of some Greek God of courage. Not an actual political comparison.

The real Churchill, not St. Winston, became Prime Minster 85 years ago after a chequered rollercoaster of a career. 

Back then was the nation obsessing over whether he was the new Palmerston, Disraeli or Gladstone? No — that politics was over eighty or seventy years ago and not much relevant or applicable.

title: It was a pleasure to join @alifortescue on @SkyNews for this debate on whether Britain should openly disagree with America not just for me, but for Baroness Neville-Jones perfectly distilling a certain security brass  view that one should always refrain from doing so. It was so realistic it reminded me of a few debates inside.
text:
It was a pleasure to join @alifortescue on @SkyNews for this debate on whether Britain should openly disagree with America not just for me, but for Baroness Neville-Jones perfectly distilling a certain security brass view that one should always refrain from doing so. It was so realistic it reminded me of a few debates inside.

Sky News Politics (@SkyNewsPolitics)

"Harold Wilson said no to joining the Vietnam War"

Ex-Foreign Office adviser Ben Judah says the UK has never "followed America everywhere".

"It caused quite a disruption", replies Baroness Neville-Jones, who was in the UK's American embassy when Wilson made the decision.

title: The Admiral has a point. We don’t really have the language to describe what we’re living through. It’s not a World War but a “series of major regional was” doesn’t quite cut it either — our time where Tel Aviv, Kyiv, Dubai, Tehran, great cities of the earth all under attack from the sky. Mass evacuations. Our time where Gaza, Pokrovsk, Kfar Aza — whole cities, towns, villages have been destroyed. Sudden, immense no fly zones. I haven’t quite found the right term but I’m searching.
text:
The Admiral has a point. We don’t really have the language to describe what we’re living through. It’s not a World War but a “series of major regional was” doesn’t quite cut it either — our time where Tel Aviv, Kyiv, Dubai, Tehran, great cities of the earth all under attack from the sky. Mass evacuations. Our time where Gaza, Pokrovsk, Kfar Aza — whole cities, towns, villages have been destroyed. Sudden, immense no fly zones. I haven’t quite found the right term but I’m searching.

BBC Radio 4 PM (@BBCPM)

"You can now say this is a global conflict."

Labour peer Admiral Lord West, former First Sea Lord, discusses the news that a US submarine has sunk an Iranian warship in the Indian Ocean using a torpedo.


Video

— https://nitter.net/BBCPM/status/2029258500258349321#m

title: RT by @b_judah: So much to enjoy in this, from haunted civil servants to Netanyahu brooding on King Herod, to understanding the absolute nightmare of British foreign policy under the shadow of Trump II
text:
So much to enjoy in this, from haunted civil servants to Netanyahu brooding on King Herod, to understanding the absolute nightmare of British foreign policy under the shadow of Trump II

Ben Judah (@b_judah)

INSIDE THE FOREIGN OFFICE

My essay on what it was like for a year to watch the rules based order collapse in the headquarters of British diplomacy @NewStatesman.

******

George Gilbert Scott’s Foreign Office – King Charles Street as its Whitehall familiars call it – is the most spectral building I’ve ever worked in. The corridor along which great officials toiled, was haunted, at least in my mind, by the telegrams sent during the chain reactions of summer 1914. The grand office was haunted by the window where Edward Grey twitched the curtain to look at the street lights, observing that August that “the lamps are going out all over Europe”. And haunt-ed too, in 2024, by the UK’s failures in Iraq, Libya and Afghanistan, where so many of those around me had begun their careers and wasted years.

Supporting the foreign secretary meant shifting through daily contradictory advice. The Middle East team pushing human rights and the UN General Assembly first. The eastern Europe team arguing Zelensky is the priority. The embassy in Washington insisting: special relationship über alles. This wasn’t anyone’s fault. Each team was a different part of Britain’s old grand strategy – international law, fighting the Kremlin and being America’s best friend – that now pulled in different directions inside Trump’s disordered new world.

You can read more here:

newstatesman.com/world/uk/20…

— https://nitter.net/b_judah/status/2029256199854596254#m

The grand office was haunted by the window where Edward Grey twitched the curtain to look at the street lights, observing that August that “the lamps are going out all over Europe”. And haunt-ed too, in 2024, by the UK’s failures in Iraq, Libya and Afghanistan, where so many of those around me had begun their careers and wasted years.

Supporting the foreign secretary meant shifting through daily contradictory advice. The Middle East team pushing human rights and the UN General Assembly first. The eastern Europe team arguing Zelensky is the priority. The embassy in Washington insisting: special relationship über alles. This wasn’t anyone’s fault. Each team was a different part of Britain’s old grand strategy – international law, fighting the Kremlin and being America’s best friend – that now pulled in different directions inside Trump’s disordered new world.

title:
RT by @mtaibbi: Last night we published a detailed timeline of the march to war with Iran. 

At Racket we link to primary sources as much as possible, and I found that it was hard to track down what Iran was saying about its own agreements with the UN's nuclear weapons watchdog (IAEA) in Western media.

Some dates that I haven't seen widely reported:

- On February 5, 2025, 16 days after he was inaugurated, President Donald Trump signed a memo that put "maximum pressure on Iran" across agencies and set terms that went beyond nuclear weapons to say that "Iran’s aggressive development of missiles, as well as other asymmetric and conventional weapons capabilities, should be countered."

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent would later say: “What we [have done] at Treasury is created a dollar shortage in the country ... the Iranian currency went into freefall, inflation exploded, and hence, we have seen the Iranian people out on the street."

- In March 2025, before negotiations between the US and Iran began over uranium enrichment that spring, Mossad began working to create a "target bank" of Iranian firepower, air superiority, nuclear developments, personnel, etc.

- On June 13, 2025, on the day Israel launched the 12-day war, one of Iran's enrichment sites was due for inspection by the IAEA. Negotiations between Iran and the U.S. about Iran's nuclear program were due to continue two days after that. 

- In November 2025, the White House National Security Strategy outlined a "predisposition to non-interventionism." The strategy also outlined a "flexible realism," with a policy of "[seeking] good relations and peaceful commercial relations with the nations of the world without imposing on them democratic or other social change that differs widely from their traditions and histories."

- On February 12, 2026, the head of the IAEA said that it was the IAEA's "firm assessment" that Iran's uranium stockpile was buried underground.

- Hours before the attack, Omani Foreign Minister al-Busaidi said that Iran was ready to give up stockpiling enriched uranium and would downblend the buried stuff into non-weapons grade.
text:
Last night we published a detailed timeline of the march to war with Iran. 

At Racket we link to primary sources as much as possible, and I found that it was hard to track down what Iran was saying about its own agreements with the UN's nuclear weapons watchdog (IAEA) in Western media.

Some dates that I haven't seen widely reported:

- On February 5, 2025, 16 days after he was inaugurated, President Donald Trump signed a memo that put "maximum pressure on Iran" across agencies and set terms that went beyond nuclear weapons to say that "Iran’s aggressive development of missiles, as well as other asymmetric and conventional weapons capabilities, should be countered."

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent would later say: “What we [have done] at Treasury is created a dollar shortage in the country ... the Iranian currency went into freefall, inflation exploded, and hence, we have seen the Iranian people out on the street."

- In March 2025, before negotiations between the US and Iran began over uranium enrichment that spring, Mossad began working to create a "target bank" of Iranian firepower, air superiority, nuclear developments, personnel, etc.

- On June 13, 2025, on the day Israel launched the 12-day war, one of Iran's enrichment sites was due for inspection by the IAEA. Negotiations between Iran and the U.S. about Iran's nuclear program were due to continue two days after that.

- In November 2025, the White House National Security Strategy outlined a "predisposition to non-interventionism." The strategy also outlined a "flexible realism," with a policy of "[seeking] good relations and peaceful commercial relations with the nations of the world without imposing on them democratic or other social change that differs widely from their traditions and histories."

- On February 12, 2026, the head of the IAEA said that it was the IAEA's "firm assessment" that Iran's uranium stockpile was buried underground.

- Hours before the attack, Omani Foreign Minister al-Busaidi said that Iran was ready to give up stockpiling enriched uranium and would downblend the buried stuff into non-weapons grade.

text:
